mechanism: Proposed mechanism: Garlic contains organosulfur compounds; alliin can be enzymatically converted to allicin, but specific clinically confirmed mechanisms for lipid/BP effects are not established well enough here to label as confirmed in humans.
safetyNotes: Common adverse effects include odor, abdominal discomfort, flatulence, nausea, and possible allergic reactions; topical raw garlic can cause chemical burns; bleeding risk is a key safety concern for supplement forms.

sources.title: Allium sativum L. - Plants of the World Online (Kew)

description: Cultivated worldwide with long historical medicinal and culinary use; used orally as food and supplements, and sometimes topically, though raw garlic on skin can cause severe irritation/chemical burns.
dosage: No single standardized dose is established across products. Use product-labeled dosing and clinical guidance, especially when bleeding risk or polypharmacy is present.
interactions: Increased bleeding risk, particularly with anticoagulants such as warfarin and aspirin; perioperative disclosure advised.
legalStatus: Widely approved as food and supplement globally (GRAS).
preparation: Food use plus supplement forms and topical forms; raw topical use is specifically cautioned due to burn risk.
region: Native range is Central Asia to northeastern Iran.
sideEffects: may cause bleeding in high doses (anticoagulant effect)
summary: Garlic is widely used as food and as a dietary supplement; human data suggest small reductions in total cholesterol/LDL, small blood-pressure reductions, and small blood-sugar reductions, while evidence for cancer risk reduction is mixed. Key caution: supplement use may increase bleeding risk, especially around surgery or with anticoagulants/antiplatelets.
